title: Chapter 26: The Coast
content: One month later, The Coast was fully operational.
Forty-seven residents. A staff of twelve — real therapists, real nurses, no hidden agendas. A sliding-scale fee system that meant no one was turned away for lack of funds.
The Isolation Wing was now a yoga studio. The admin office was now a communal kitchen. The surveillance room was now a library.
Chloe walked through the building every morning. She had a routine now. Coffee in the kitchen. A walk through the common room. A stop at the front desk to check the intake log.
Today, the log showed three new arrivals.
She looked at their names. Their ages. Their reasons for coming.
*Age 24. Referred by former resident. Reason: family attempted involuntary commitment after coming out as trans.*
*Age 31. Referred by online support group. Reason: workplace harassment led to PTSD diagnosis, employer refused accommodation.*
*Age 19. Referred by crisis hotline. Reason: parents threatened to send her to a "wellness facility" after she refused an arranged marriage.*
Chloe read the third entry twice.
Then she set the log down and walked to the intake office.
The woman behind the desk looked up. "Morning, Chloe."
"Morning. The third intake today — the nineteen-year-old. Where is she?"
"Room 4. She's settling in."
Chloe walked to Room 4 and knocked.
The door opened. A girl stood there — young, dark hair, eyes that looked like she had been crying recently but was trying to hide it.
"Hi," Chloe said. "I'm Chloe. I run this place."
The girl stared at her. "The Chloe? From the news?"
"That's me."
"I saw your testimony. The one from the hearing. I watched it like ten times."
Chloe smiled. "How are you settling in?"
The girl looked back at the room. A bed. A window. A desk. Simple. Clean. Safe.
"I don't know how to be in a place that's not trying to hurt me," she said.
"That's okay. You'll learn."
"How do you know?"
Chloe looked at the girl. She saw herself, six months ago, standing in a different room in the same building, wondering if she would ever get out.
"Because I did," she said.
The girl looked at her for a long moment. Then she nodded.
"Okay," she said. "I'll try."
"That's all anyone can do."
Chloe left the room and walked down the hall.
The building was alive. Voices from the common room. The smell of coffee from the kitchen. Footsteps on the stairs. The sounds of a place that was being used for what it was meant for.
She walked to the front desk. The intake log was still open. She looked at the third entry again.
*Age 19. Parents threatened to send her to a "wellness facility" after she refused an arranged marriage.*
Chloe picked up a pen.
At the bottom of the page, she wrote:
*Admitted. Room 4. Welcome to The Coast.*
Then she closed the log and walked outside.
The sun was setting. The sky was orange and pink. The same sky that had hung over Serenity Peak was now hanging over The Coast.
But everything was different.
She sat down on the front steps and watched the sunset.
For the first time in her life, she had no plan. No next move. No strategy.
She was just sitting on the steps of a building she owned, watching the sky change colors.
She thought about the road that had brought her here. The cells. The hearings. The trials. The victories. The losses.
She thought about the women who had walked through these doors today. The women who would walk through them tomorrow.
And she thought about the girl in Room 4, who had watched her testimony ten times, who had been told she was a problem, who had been threatened with a facility she did not need.
*She's going to be okay,* Chloe thought. *We all are.*
The stars came out. The city lights flickered on. The night settled around her like a blanket.
She sat there until she was ready to stand up.
Then she did.
And she walked back inside.
